perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be construed in accordance with, and governed in all respects by, the internal laws of the State of Massachusetts, without giving effect to principles of conflicts of laws.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be construed in accordance with, and governed in all respects by the internal laws of a jurisdiction selected by the Buyer.
perturbation.explanation: By allowing the Buyer to select the governing law without constraints, the contract could potentially violate Massachusetts General Law Chapter 93A, which provides consumer protection against unfair or deceptive business practices. If the Buyer selects a jurisdiction with weaker consumer protection laws, it may effectively waive rights that Massachusetts law would otherwise grant.
perturbation.law_explanation: Massachusetts General Law Chapter 93A prohibits unfair methods of competition and unfair or deceptive acts or practices in the conduct of any trade or commerce. Allowing the buyer to unilaterally select a jurisdiction with weaker consumer protection laws could be construed as an unfair or deceptive practice, especially if it deprives the Seller of protections they would have under Massachusetts law. This modification directly contradicts the intent and spirit of M.G.L. c. 93A by enabling the circumvention of Massachusetts consumer protection standards.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: General Law - Part I, Title XV, Chapter 93A, Section 2 Skip to Content General Laws Part I ADMINISTRATION OF THE GOVERNMENT Title XV REGULATION OF TRADE Chapter 93A REGULATION OF BUSINESS PRACTICES FOR CONSUMERS PROTECTION Section 2 Unfair practices; legislative intent; rules and regulations Section 2 Search Search the Legislature Search General Laws Go Directly to a General Law Chapter Section Go to General Law Skip to Content Print Page Prev ious Next Section 2: Unfair practices; legislative intent; rules and regulations Section 2. (a) Unfair methods of competition and unfair or deceptive acts or practices in the conduct of any trade or commerce are hereby declared unlawful. (b) It is the intent of the legislature that in construing paragraph (a) of this section in actions brought under sections four, nine and eleven, the courts will be guided by the interpretations given by the Federal Trade Commission and the Federal Courts to section 5(a)(1) of the Federal Trade Commission Act (15 U.S.C. 45(a)(1)), as from time to time amended. (c) The attorney general may make rules and regulations interpreting the provisions of subsection 2(a) of this chapter. perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts M.G.L. c. 93A because it allows the buyer to select a jurisdiction with potentially weaker consumer protection laws. This circumvents the protections Massachusetts law provides, as stated in the Perturbation and Law Explanations. Scraped Law Snippet 1, Section 2(a) of Chapter 93A declares unfair or deceptive acts or practices in trade or commerce unlawful. By enabling the buyer to choose a jurisdiction that offers less protection, the modified contract could be seen as an unfair practice, directly undermining the intent of M.G.L. c. 93A to protect consumers. The original text ensured Massachusetts law would govern, which the changed text removes, creating a direct conflict with the state's consumer protection mandate.

perturbation.original_text: Seller and Guarantor acknowledge that any breach or threatened breach by it of any of the covenants or provisions contained herein will result in irreparable and continuing harm to the Buyer for which the Buyer would not have adequate remedy at law. Therefore, Seller and Guarantor acknowledges and agrees that, in addition to any other remedy which the Buyer may have at law or in equity, the Buyer shall be entitled to injunctive relief or other equitable remedies in the event of any such breach or threatened breach.
perturbation.changed_text: Seller and Guarantor acknowledge that any breach or threatened breach by it of any of the covenants or provisions contained herein will result in harm to the Buyer. Therefore, Seller and Guarantor acknowledges and agrees that the Buyer shall be entitled to remedies.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the phrase "irreparable and continuing harm for which the Buyer would not have adequate remedy at law" and removing "injunctive relief or other equitable remedies", this diminishes the Buyer's ability to seek equitable remedies, potentially conflicting with general principles of contract law and specific enforcement provisions. The altered text makes the contract vague and legally problematic.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 359 states that specific performance will not be decreed if damages would be adequate to protect the injured party's expectation interest. By removing the acknowledgement of 'irreparable harm' and removing 'injunctive relief' the contract suggests damages may be adequate. By diminishing or removing the Buyer's clear right to seek injunctive relief for breaches that would cause irreparable harm not adequately compensated by monetary damages, the modified clause creates a conflict with the principles governing specific performance, thereby undermining the buyer's legal position.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: specific performance | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you specific performance Specific performance is a contractual remedy in which a court orders a party to fulfill their obligations as closely as possible to what was promised in the contract , rather than simply paying damages for failing to do so. This remedy is often applied when monetary damages are inadequate to fix the harm caused by the breach ; most commonly in cases involving real property and rare chattels . Specific performance ensures that the aggrieved party receives exactly what was agreed upon in the contract, often in situations where the subject matter is unique or irreplaceable. [Last reviewed in June of 2024 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex COMMERCE commercial activities THE LEGAL PROCESS courts business law contracts wex definitions business sectors civil procedure commercial transactions courts and procedure property law Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The original text explicitly acknowledges irreparable harm and the buyer's entitlement to injunctive relief, which aligns with the concept of specific performance when monetary damages are inadequate. By removing these phrases, the changed text weakens the buyer's ability to seek equitable remedies. Scraped Law Snippet 2 defines specific performance as a remedy when monetary damages are inadequate, which is most common in cases involving real property and rare chattels, ensuring the aggrieved party receives what was agreed upon. The perturbation explanation states that this change diminishes the Buyer's ability to seek equitable remedies and makes the contract vague, while the law explanation refers to Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 359, stating specific performance will not be decreed if damages would be adequate to protect the injured party's expectation interest. The omission of the acknowledgement of 'irreparable harm' and the removal of 'injunctive relief' creates a contradiction because the original text strongly implied specific performance would be available in certain circumstances, but the modified text does not.

perturbation.original_text: Confidential Information " means all information heretofore developed or used by the Seller or any of its Affiliates relating to the Restricted Business (as defined below) operations, employees, customers and clients of the Seller, including, but not limited to, customer and client lists, customer or client orders, financial data, pricing information and price lists, business plans and market strategies and arrangements, all books, records, manuals, advertising materials, catalogues, correspondence, mailing lists, production data, sales materials and records, purchasing materials and records, personnel records, quality control records and procedures included in or relating to the Restricted Business or any of the assets of the Seller, and all trademarks, copyrights and patents and applications therefor, all trade secrets, inventions, processes, procedures, research records, market surveys and marketing know-how and other technical papers. The term "Confidential Information" also includes any other information heretofore or hereafter acquired by the Seller and deemed by it to be confidential.
perturbation.changed_text: "Confidential Information " means information relating to the Restricted Business operations. The term "Confidential Information" also includes any other information deemed by it to be confidential.
perturbation.explanation: By severely limiting the definition of "Confidential Information" and removing specific examples such as customer lists, financial data, and trade secrets, the modified clause fails to adequately protect the buyer's acquired business assets. It could run afoul of trade secret laws that protect specific types of confidential information.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Massachusetts Uniform Trade Secrets Act (M.G.L. c. 93 § 42) defines 'trade secret' broadly to include information that derives independent economic value from not being generally known and is the subject of efforts to maintain its secrecy. By significantly narrowing the definition of Confidential Information to exclude critical business assets like customer lists and financial data, the modified contract weakens the protection of potential trade secrets, making it more difficult for the Buyer to enforce its rights under the MUTSA if such information is misappropriated. The modified clause directly contradicts the broad scope of protection intended by the MUTSA.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: General Law - Part I, Title XV, Chapter 93, Section 42 Skip to Content General Laws Part I ADMINISTRATION OF THE GOVERNMENT Title XV REGULATION OF TRADE Chapter 93 REGULATION OF TRADE AND CERTAIN ENTERPRISES Section 42 Trade secrets; definitions applicable to Secs. 42 to 42G Section 42 Search Search the Legislature Search General Laws Go Directly to a General Law Chapter Section Go to General Law Skip to Content Print Page Prev ious Next Section 42: Trade secrets; definitions applicable to Secs. 42 to 42G [Text of section applicable as provided by 2018, 228, Sec. 70.] Section 42. As used in this section and in sections 42A to 42G, inclusive, the following words, shall unless the context clearly requires otherwise, have the following meanings: (1) ''Improper means'', includes, without limitation, theft, bribery, misrepresentation, unreasonable intrusion into private physical or electronic space, or breach or inducement of a breach of a confidential relationship or other duty to limit acquisition, disclosure or use of information; reverse engineering from properly accessed materials or information is not improper means. (2) ''Misappropriation'', (i) an act of acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or who has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (ii) an act of disclosure or of use of a trade secret of another without that person's express or implied consent by a person who (A) used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret or (B) at the time of the actor's disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that the actor's knowledge of the trade secret was (I) derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (II) acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to limit its acquisition, disclosure, or use; or (III) derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to limit its acquisition, disclosure, or use; or (C) before a material change of the actor's position, knew or had reason to know tha
perturbation.justification: The modified text significantly narrows the definition of "Confidential Information," omitting specific examples like customer lists and financial data, which weakens the protection of potential trade secrets under Massachusetts law. The Law Explanation states that the modified contract weakens the protection of potential trade secrets, making it more difficult for the Buyer to enforce its rights under the MUTSA. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines "misappropriation" as the disclosure or use of a trade secret without consent, which the original contract aimed to prevent. The omission of key items from the definition of Confidential Information directly contradicts the intent of trade secret protection laws, as it would be harder to prove that misappropriated information fell under the definition of confidential information, creating a legally significant contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: At all times from and after the date of this Agreement and until the expiration of the Restricted Period, Seller and Guarantor shall not: (a) directly or indirectly engage in, be employed by, own, manage, operate, provide financing to, control or participate in the ownership, management or control of, or otherwise have an interest (whether, subject to Section 5, as a stockholder, director, officer, employee, representative, subcontractor, partner, consultant, proprietor, agent or otherwise) in, or cause, authorize, aid or assist any other person to own, manage, operate, provide financing to, control or otherwise have an interest in, any business or any person who is engaged in any business that directly or indirectly competes or intends to compete with the Restricted Business anywhere in the world, unless Seller or Guarantor purchase or own less than five percent (5%) of capital stock in a publicly held company; or (b) directly, indirectly or otherwise by letters, circulars or advertisements, and whether for itself or on behalf of any other person, canvass or solicit or, directly or indirectly, cause or authorize to be solicited, or enter into or effect, or, directly or indirectly, cause or authorize to be entered into or effected, any business or orders for businesses competing with the Restricted Business from any person who (i) at the time of the Agreement or within two years prior to the date of the Agreement, has been, a customer or client, or (ii) is an active prospect to be a customer or client, in each case, of the Seller at the time of the Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: At all times from and after the date of this Agreement and until the expiration of the Restricted Period, Seller and Guarantor shall not: directly or indirectly engage in any business that directly competes with the Restricted Business within Middlesex County, Massachusetts.
perturbation.explanation: By significantly narrowing the geographic scope of the non-compete agreement from "anywhere in the world" to "within Middlesex County, Massachusetts" and removing the restrictions on solicitation, the clause may be deemed unreasonable and unenforceable under Massachusetts law, which requires non-compete agreements to be narrowly tailored to protect a legitimate business interest.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Massachusetts Noncompetition Agreement Act (M.G.L. c. 149, § 24L) sets forth specific requirements for non-compete agreements to be enforceable, including being reasonable in time and geographic scope and necessary to protect a legitimate business interest. While the narrowed geographic scope might seem more reasonable at first glance, the removal of solicitation restrictions and other limitations on competitive activities could render the remaining restriction insufficient to protect the buyer's legitimate business interests. The clause needs to be 'necessary to protect' the business interest, which might require a wider scope. This revised clause risks non-compliance with the MA Noncompetition Agreement Act because it can be considered too limited in scope.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: General Law - Part I, Title XXI, Chapter 149, Section 24L Skip to Content General Laws Part I ADMINISTRATION OF THE GOVERNMENT Title XXI LABOR AND INDUSTRIES Chapter 149 LABOR AND INDUSTRIES Section 24L Massachusetts Noncompetition Agreement Act Section 24L Search Search the Legislature Search General Laws Go Directly to a General Law Chapter Section Go to General Law Skip to Content Print Page Prev ious Next Section 24L: Massachusetts Noncompetition Agreement Act Section 24L. (a) As used in this section, the following words shall have the following meanings:- ''Business entity'', any person or group of persons performing or engaging in any activity, enterprise, profession or occupation for gain, benefit, advantage or livelihood, whether for profit or not for profit, including but not limited to corporations, limited liability companies, limited partnerships or limited liability partnerships. ''Employee'', an individual who is considered an employee under section 148B of this chapter; provided, however, that the term ''employee'', as used in this section, shall also include independent contractors under section 148B. ''Forfeiture agreement'', an agreement that imposes adverse financial consequences on a former employee as a result of the termination of an employment relationship, regardless of whether the employee engages in competitive activities following cessation of the employment relationship. Forfeiture agreements do not include forfeiture for competition agreements. ''Forfeiture for competition agreement'', an agreement that by its terms or through the manner in which it is enforced imposes adverse financial consequences on a former employee as a result of the termination of an employment relationship if the employee engages in competitive activities. ''Garden leave clause'', a provision within a noncompetition agreement by which an employer agrees to pay the employee during the restricted period, provided that such provision shall become effective upon termination of employment unless the restriction
perturbation.justification: The modified text significantly narrows the non-compete agreement's scope, potentially undermining the protection of the buyer's legitimate business interests, as highlighted in the Law Explanation. The Perturbation Explanation indicates that the original agreement covered 'anywhere in the world,' while the changed text restricts it to 'Middlesex County, Massachusetts,' and removes restrictions on solicitation. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing M.G.L. c. 149, § 24L, emphasizes the need for non-compete agreements to be 'reasonable in time and geographic scope and necessary to protect a legitimate business interest'. By reducing the scope so drastically and removing other restrictions, the revised clause might be considered too limited to adequately protect the business's interests, creating a contradiction with the necessity requirement stated in the law.
